title: Lavender and Epsom salt bath for muscle tension
subtitle: A magnesium-rich relaxing bath for tight, tense muscles
excerpt: Epsom salts dissolved in a warm bath with a lavender infusion. The magnesium in Epsom salts is absorbed transdermally and has a well-established relaxing effect on muscle tissue. Lavender adds nervous system calming and a pleasant scent. One of the most effective and accessible home preparations for muscular tension.
sourceNotes: Traditional use of Epsom salt baths for muscle relaxation documented across 19th and 20th century hydrotherapy literature. Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), lavender entry, nervine and relaxing properties, aromatic use for anxiety and muscular tension.

content.text: Epsom salts dissolved in a warm bath alongside a lavender infusion. Epsom salts are magnesium sulphate, the magnesium is absorbed through the skin during the bath via

and acts as a natural muscle relaxant. Muscles need magnesium to contract and to release. Low magnesium is linked to cramping and tightness. Restoring the level in the tissue through the bath helps reduce soreness. The lavender infusion adds nervous-system calm: the scented oils are taken up through the skin and breathed in with the steam. It turns the salt bath from a plain soak into a much more pleasant one. The combination is one of the most effective things in the home herbal kit for sore muscles after exercise, desk-work tightness, or the full-body ache of a stressful week.

attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a medical professional for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Kidney conditions: Epsom salt baths are generally safe. People with impaired kidney function should seek medical advice before regular Epsom salt baths, since magnesium taken up through the skin adds to the body's magnesium load.

Bath temperature: keep the bath warm rather than very hot for a muscle-relaxing effect. Very hot water can cause faintness on standing, so stand up slowly from the bath.

Seek medical care for muscle pain that is unexplained, severe, with a fever, after a fall or injury, or not lifting with rest and home care.
attrs.title: Important safety notes

items.name: Epsom salts
items.prepNote: pharmaceutical or food-grade magnesium sulphate crystals

items.name: Dried lavender flowers
items.prepNote: for a strong lavender infusion to add to the bath

items.name: Water
items.prepNote: just off the boil, for the lavender infusion only

content.text: Place {{dried-lavender-flowers}} of dried lavender in the large saucepan. Pour {{water}} of just-off-boil water over, lid, and steep for 10 minutes. Strain through a sieve.
Fill the bath and add both

Run a warm bath. Pour {{epsom-salts}} of Epsom salts into the water as it fills and stir to dissolve. Add the strained lavender infusion. Swirl. Soak for 15 to 20 minutes. The warm water, magnesium, and lavender work together to release muscle tension during the soak itself.

One bath as needed, after exercise, after a long day at a desk, or during a period of high stress and muscular tension. Can be used two to three times a week. Rest for at least 30 minutes after the bath for maximum effect.
When NOT to use this remedy
Discuss with your doctor if you have kidney disease. See your doctor for severe unexplained muscle pain, pain following injury, or muscle pain accompanied by weakness or swelling.
